All right, giving a raise. So many questions about this topic. So let's dive in. Okay, the primary factor is going to determine a raise and supersedes all others, is that you demonstrate excellence in your work, or that somebody on your team is demonstrating excellence. Right? And so the highest performers will usually earn raises the fastest, right? So let's talk about situations that can lead to a raise or would make one necessary or suggestible. Okay? One is increase in responsibilities. So you officially have made changes to their for our documents, you've added responsibilities, maybe they're overseeing people now, okay, something like that. Two is they move into management, or they're overseeing the team in some way. Maybe it's management, maybe it's being the point of contact for vendors, whatever. Three is a cost of living increase over time. So this is something that you can get done on like salary.com for an analysis, et cetera. But as the cost of living goes up in an area, you need to make sure that you're accommodating, otherwise you will lose people because they simply can't afford their lifestyle anymore because you're underpaying in the market that you're in. Right? Consistent hard work and achievement. Obviously, if somebody's always meeting, going above and beyond expectations and exceeding expectations on their quarterly reviews, it's like, okay, after a couple quarters, like maybe we need to give them a raise, right? And lastly is the acquisition of new skill and talent. That is typically difficult to replicate or attain, right? So maybe that person has now increased their closing percentage by 35%, which is insane, right? Okay, well, can you pay them a little bit more per close now that they're closing at a higher rate, right? Because they're making the business more money, so they are more valuable to the business, therefore you need to exchange that value by then providing them more value back to themselves, right? So frequency and percentage of raises. We don't give raises based on any sort of preset cadence in our business, nor are raises equal within the company or departments, and raises are honestly based on a case-by-case basis or an individual basis. And so I suggest saying the same things. If you are a small business and you do not have a ladder to climb, you don't have a preset career path for somebody, then you need to let them know that. And so what the most important thing is, is that they understand that it's not going to be laid out clearly to them and that they have to communicate with you in understanding how it works, right? And so some common times that raises are given, could be at a quarterly review during a promotion, when someone has an increase in direct reports or an increase in responsibility, right? So what to expect as an amount for giving a raise, right? Here are the averages across the US. Standard performance and not meeting above criteria is one to 3%. It's basically like you're doing your job, I don't want you to leave, but I don't think you're doing, like you're not exceeding expectations all the time. One to 3%. Consistent performance and reliability is about four to 5%. That means that person is always reliable, they're crushing it, they're a high performer, they're doing well. Third is excellence, high performance and added responsibilities. That merits about five to 10%. So that means like that person's a key player, you do not wanna lose them, you want to retain them, right? Like you're not gonna have an easy time replacing that person. And lastly is a promotion to management, new skill acquisition or immense increase in responsibilities, like you 50% increase on the responsibility list, right? And that's gonna be a 10 to 20%, right? Which is pretty life changing for somebody. So how are you going to ask or get a raise, right? And so this is something that I urge you to communicate with your team and this works incredibly well for us, I hope it works for you as well. I just like to be transparent. So this is what I let our team know. Okay, you need to start by understanding your average job cost, salary and cost of living so that you can understand an accurate pay range for your position. So if somebody were to come to me and be like, I wanna make 70 grand a year in this position, I'm like, bro, the highest end of your position right now is about 55 grand out of the whole US. Like, and you wanna get paid 70, please tell me how. You know, and so it doesn't look good coming to someone saying you want a raise that's unreasonable because you haven't done the research. A second is once you know that number, based on the criteria you meet, know what is a proper percentage to ask for. So looking back at these percentages, which one do you think is appropriate based on what you've done, right? Three, do not bombard your supervisor or come into the conversation emotional. Instead, you wanna start with something like, you know, I really enjoy working here and I find my project super challenging. In the last year, you know, I felt like this girl, my work has expanded quite a bit and I believe my roles and responsibilities and contributions have risen. I really like to discuss the possibilities of reviewing my compensation with you. Is there a time in the next seven days that would work well? That's very, it gives the person, you're not asking them on the spot to do anything, you're not being demanding and you're not being emotional, okay? That's a great way to approach a manager or supervisor about discussing arrays. You want to be specific as to what range you want and why you should have it. So that's exactly why you need to do your research, okay? You need to be prepared to say something like, after a lot of research, which I have here if you'd like to see it, and after how much I feel I've contributed to the company, I would like you to consider an increase of five to 7,000 per year. It has been six or eight months since my salary was last reviewed. I greatly appreciate you considering this, right? It's very logical, it's very adult. Next, bring with you your list of achievements and areas your supervisor has suggested for improvement that you've acted upon, okay? So you're saying, hey, you've asked me to improve here, I've improved here, here's all the things I did for the business, here's how it affected the bottom line, like showing your value to your supervisor, right? And then lastly, a couple of things, which is don't be aggressive or emotional, okay? This will just give the opposite effect of what you want because the last thing someone wants is you to come in with all these emotions, being like, I don't know why I haven't had a raise yet, like what's wrong with you, you don't think I'm valuable, like none of that's going to work. Come in logically with your case, basically, you're selling them on why you deserve a raise. And lastly, be patient. Your manager needs to think it through, it's not an easy decision for them, it's something that they really need to review your case, review their numbers, review the business numbers, discuss it with their partners, maybe it's their spouse, their operations manager, and then don't expect it to be overnight and remain patient while you expect a response. So maybe it's three days, maybe it's seven days. Sit patiently knowing that you've done everything on your end and now it's on their end, the ball's in their court.
